resources.string: How much a company pays in dividends each year relative to its stock price.
Percentage of a company's earnings paid out as dividends each year.
The company's earnings divided by the total number of shares.
The stock price divided by its earnings per share.
Quarterly earnings growth compared to the same quarter of the previous year (YoY).
The estimated intrinsic value calculated using the configured valuation floor (P/E).
Expected earnings growth for the next year.
Estimated payback period in years, based on the current price and estimated earnings growth.
Price to earnings ratio divided by the forward earnings growth estimate.
Total cash per share divided by EPS.\n\nIndicates how many years of earnings are held in cash.
Net income divided by shareholders' equity.\n\nMeasures how efficiently a company uses equity to generate profit.
Net income divided by revenue.\n\nIndicates how much profit a company retains from each dollar of revenue.
Total cash and equivalents divided by shares outstanding.
Total debt divided by shareholders' equity.\n\nMeasures a company's financial leverage.
Current assets divided by current liabilities.\n\nA ratio above 1 indicates the company can cover its short-term obligations.
Difference between the earnings yield and the 10-year Treasury yield.\n\nA positive spread suggests equities offer better expected returns than bonds.
Yield on 10-year U.S. Treasury bonds.\n\nUsed as the risk-free rate benchmark for U.S. markets.
Average yield on 10-year Euro Area government bonds.\n\nUsed as the risk-free rate benchmark for European markets.
